items.q: Does HDL cholesterol raise heart disease risk?
items.correct: No - HDL carries cholesterol back to the liver and is linked with lower risk; high LDL is linked with higher risk.
items.misconception: All cholesterol is bad and raises heart disease risk.
items.random_wrong: HDL turns into LDL once it reaches the arteries.
items.why: Students treat 'cholesterol' as one harmful substance rather than distinguishing LDL and HDL.

items.q: Does a correlation between smoking and heart disease prove smoking causes it?
items.correct: A correlation alone does not prove causation; other factors could be involved, though further evidence can support a causal link.
items.misconception: If two variables are correlated, one must cause the other.
items.random_wrong: Correlation means the two variables are completely unrelated.
items.why: Students equate a correlation with proof of cause and effect.

items.correct: It is the build-up of fatty plaques (atheroma) in artery walls, narrowing the lumen and restricting blood flow.
items.misconception: Atherosclerosis is simply the heart getting weaker with age.
items.random_wrong: Atherosclerosis is an infection of the blood vessels.
items.why: Students confuse a structural fatty build-up with general heart weakening or infection.

items.correct: fatty plaque build-up that narrows arteries
items.misconception: an infection of the heart muscle
items.random_wrong: a thickening of the blood itself
items.why: The answer examiners want is: fatty plaque build-up that narrows arteries. “an infection of the heart muscle” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A high blood level of which lipoprotein is associated with greater cardiovascular risk?
items.why: It's LDL. “HDL” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A study finds smokers have more heart attacks. This correlation:
items.correct: does not by itself prove smoking causes heart attacks
items.misconception: proves smoking has no effect on the heart
items.why: It's does not by itself prove smoking causes heart attacks. “proves smoking has no effect on the heart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: HDL is sometimes called 'good cholesterol' because it:
items.correct: carries cholesterol from tissues back to the liver
items.why: The answer examiners want is: carries cholesterol from tissues back to the liver. “deposits cholesterol into artery walls” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
